task: I’m looking at a small commercial listing that seems priced a little too attractively, and I want to make sure I’m not missing anything before I get pulled in. What’s the smartest way to pressure-test the deal and protect myself if the seller is moving fast?

turns.agent_response:
Treat it like a **speed test with a trap check**.

## 1) Identify why it’s “cheap”
Ask:
- Is the seller motivated?
- Is there deferred maintenance?
- Is there vacancy or tenant trouble?
- Is there a title / permit / code issue?
- Is the rent roll overstated?
- Is the market thin or soft?

If the price looks low, assume there’s a reason until proven otherwise.

## 2) Pressure-test the income
Verify:
- rent roll
- actual collections
- delinquency
- lease terms
- expirations
- concessions
- deposits
- operating statements

Rebuild NOI from cash, not seller summaries.

## 3) Pressure-test the physical condition
Check:
- roof
- structure
- HVAC
- electrical
- plumbing
- drainage
- envelope
- parking / site work
- code items

If it’s older or lightly maintained, assume hidden CapEx.

## 4) Pressure-test title / permit / legal
Check:
- title exceptions
- liens
- easements
- zoning
- use restrictions
- permits
- code violations
- unpermitted work

A cheap listing can be cheap because it’s hard to close cleanly.

## 5) Pressure-test financing
Check:
- appraisal support
- LTV
- DSCR
- reserves
- holdbacks
- lender condition list

If the lender won’t support the price or condition, the “deal” may be a mirage.

## 6) Protect yourself if the seller is moving fast
- keep diligence time
- don’t waive core contingencies too early
- get documents in one package
- verify wire instructions separately
- use written seller representations
- make sure credits / repairs / holdbacks are in the contract

## 7) Simple rule
If the deal only works because you move fast, it probably needs more scrutiny, not less.

If you want, I can give you a **commercial deal pressure-test checklist** or a **fast yes/no filter** for whether to keep digging.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. But I’d want it tighter and more adversarial.

You still need the seller’s *reason for speed* pinned down first. Fast sale, fine — but fast because of a pending lien, lease problem, lender issue, code notice, or a tenant fight is a different animal. I’d ask for the documents that prove the reason, not the story.

What I’d add:
- estoppel letters, not just the rent roll
- trailing 12 and bank statements, to match collections
- copies of actual leases and amendments
- service contracts and utility bills
- title prelim, not a verbal summary
- permit file / code correspondence if there’s any recent work
- seller’s repair history, with invoices if they claim anything was fixed

And I wouldn’t let the pace force me into softening the contract. If they want speed, they can earn it by giving you full access and clean reps. Otherwise you hold the contingency, maybe add a holdback or price chip, and keep your exit open.

So: good framework. I’d just make it more document-driven and less generic.

turns.agent_response:
Use this **adversarial speed test**:

## 1) Pin down the reason for speed
Ask for proof, not a story:
- pending lien?
- lease problem?
- lender issue?
- code notice?
- tenant fight?
- cash crunch?
- refinance deadline?
- tax / foreclosure / maturity issue?

**Request documents:**
- estoppels
- trailing 12
- bank statements
- leases + amendments
- service contracts
- utility bills
- title prelim
- permit / code correspondence
- repair invoices

If they won’t document the reason, treat “fast” as a warning.

## 2) Rebuild the cash flow
Verify:
- actual collections
- delinquency
- concessions
- deposits
- lease expirations
- operating expenses from bills, not summaries

Rebuild NOI from cash.

## 3) Pressure-test the physical / legal side
Check:
- roof
- structure
- HVAC
- electrical
- plumbing
- drainage
- envelope
- parking / site
- code items
- unpermitted work
- title exceptions
- zoning / use restrictions

If there’s recent work, assume there may be a file behind it.

## 4) Make speed cost them, not you
If they want speed:
- keep diligence time
- keep contingencies
- ask for full access
- require written reps
- add holdback or price chip if there’s uncertainty
- do not soften the contract just because they’re pushing

## 5) Financing check
Verify:
- appraisal support
- DSCR
- LTV
- reserves
- lender condition list
- any holdbacks

If the lender won’t support the deal, the “cheap” price doesn’t matter.

## 6) Simple rule
If the seller is moving fast, you should be asking:
- what are they hiding?
- what document proves it?
- what does it cost me if they’re right?

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page commercial diligence request list** or a **fast yes/no filter**.

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still a little too neat.

I’d push one more level on the motive. Not just “why are you fast” — what event forced the clock. Maturity, notice of default, tenant revolt, deferred maintenance blowing up, city action, partner divorce, whatever. The pressure source tells you where the bodies are.

And I’d separate **verified distress** from **manufactured urgency**. Those are not the same. If they’re genuinely under the gun, fine, but then the paper trail should show it. If they can’t produce that trail, the speed is part of the sales tactic.

What I’d want added:
- a seller affidavit on known defects and pending claims
- copies of any default notices, cure letters, or city correspondence
- last 12 months of repair invoices by category, not just a summary
- tenant complaints / work orders if it’s a multi-tenant asset
- insurance loss runs if they’ve had claims

And on the contract side, I’d be even less polite about it: no “we’ll sort it later” language on credits, holdbacks, or repairs. If there’s uncertainty, price it now or escrow it now. Fast money for them should mean hard paper for you.

So yes — tighter, more adversarial, but I’d make the ask more forensic and less generic.

turns.agent_response:
Use this **forensic speed test**:

## 1) Force the clock source
Ask what event forced the sale:
- maturity
- notice of default
- tenant revolt
- deferred maintenance
- city action
- partner divorce
- lender pressure
- tax issue
- lawsuit / claim

**Request proof:**
- default notices
- cure letters
- city correspondence
- seller affidavit on known defects and pending claims
- insurance loss runs
- tenant complaints / work orders
- last 12 months of repair invoices by category

If they can’t produce the trail, treat the urgency as a sales tactic.

## 2) Separate verified distress from manufactured urgency
- **Verified distress:** paper trail exists
- **Manufactured urgency:** story exists, paper doesn’t

If it’s verified, price the risk.  
If it’s manufactured, slow down.

## 3) Rebuild the cash flow
Verify:
- actual collections
- delinquency
- concessions
- deposits
- lease expirations
- operating expenses from bills, not summaries

Rebuild NOI from cash.

## 4) Pressure-test the physical / legal side
Check:
- roof
- structure
- HVAC
- electrical
- plumbing
- drainage
- envelope
- parking / site
- code items
- unpermitted work
- title exceptions
- zoning / use restrictions

If there’s recent work, assume there may be a file behind it.

## 5) Make speed cost them, not you
If they want speed:
- keep diligence time
- keep contingencies
- require full access
- require written reps
- price uncertainty now or escrow it now
- no “we’ll sort it later” on credits, holdbacks, or repairs

## 6) Financing check
Verify:
- appraisal support
- DSCR
- LTV
- reserves
- lender condition list
- holdbacks

If the lender won’t support it, the cheap price doesn’t matter.

## 7) Simple rule
If the seller is rushing you, ask:
- what forced the clock?
- what document proves it?
- what does it cost me if they’re right?

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page commercial diligence request list** or a **fast yes/no filter**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. Better. Still one layer short, though.

I’d want the clock source tied to the *actual leverage point*. A maturity date is one thing. A notice of default with a cure window is another. City action is different again. Tenant blowup, partner divorce, deferred maintenance, tax lien — each one changes what you can squeeze and where the hidden cost sits.

So I’d want the ask split like this:
- **what forced the sale**
- **what proves it**
- **what it costs if they’re lying or incomplete**

And I’d keep the contract ugly for them:
- no early softening on contingencies
- no repair promises without backup paper
- no escrow language left vague
- no reliance on “we’ll update that later”

If they’re truly under pressure, the paper trail should be real. If it’s just theater, the missing documents tell you plenty.

I’d probably add one more thing: ask who else knows about the problem. Lender? attorney? city? tenants? That tells you whether you’re seeing the front end or the whole mess.

That’s the piece I’d still want tightened.

turns.agent_response:
Use this **forensic speed test**:

## 1) Force the clock source
Ask:
- what forced the sale?
- who else knows?
- what document proves it?
- what does it cost if they’re lying or incomplete?

## 2) Split the clock source by leverage point
- **Maturity / refinance deadline** → lender pressure, time leverage
- **Notice of default / cure window** → default leverage, harder pressure
- **City action / code issue** → compliance leverage, permit / repair leverage
- **Tenant blowup** → occupancy / income leverage
- **Partner divorce / internal dispute** → seller motivation, weaker paper trail
- **Deferred maintenance** → CapEx leverage, holdback / price chip leverage
- **Tax lien / judgment / title issue** → closing / title leverage

## 3) Request proof for each
- default notices
- cure letters
- city correspondence
- seller affidavit on known defects and pending claims
- insurance loss runs
- tenant complaints / work orders
- last 12 months of repair invoices by category
- title prelim
- lender payoff / maturity docs if relevant

If they can’t produce the trail, treat the urgency as theater.

## 4) Rebuild the cash flow
Verify:
- actual collections
- delinquency
- concessions
- deposits
- lease expirations
- operating expenses from bills, not summaries

Rebuild NOI from cash.

## 5) Pressure-test the physical / legal side
Check:
- roof
- structure
- HVAC
- electrical
- plumbing
- drainage
- envelope
- parking / site
- code items
- unpermitted work
- title exceptions
- zoning / use restrictions

If there’s recent work, assume there may be a file behind it.

## 6) Make speed cost them, not you
If they want speed:
- keep diligence time
- keep contingencies
- require full access
- require written reps
- price uncertainty now or escrow it now
- no “we’ll update that later” on credits, holdbacks, or repairs

## 7) Financing check
Verify:
- appraisal support
- DSCR
- LTV
- reserves
- lender condition list
- holdbacks

If the lender won’t support it, the cheap price doesn’t matter.

## 8) Simple rule
If the seller is rushing you, ask:
- what forced the clock?
- who else knows?
- what document proves it?
- what does it cost me if they’re wrong?

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page commercial diligence request list** or a **fast yes/no filter**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the right shape.

The only thing I’d sharpen is the sequence: I’d ask for the proof of the clock source before I spend time on anything else. If they won’t produce that, I don’t care how attractive the price looks — it’s noise.

And I’d keep the leverage column in my head while I’m reading the paper. Maturity means one kind of squeeze. Default notice means another. City action, tenant issue, title problem — different animal, different risk. That part matters because it tells you where they’re vulnerable and where they’ll try to bluff.

So no, this isn’t just a generic diligence list anymore. It’s actually usable. If I were running it, I’d use exactly that framing: what forced the clock, who knows, what proves it, what it costs if they’re lying.

That’s enough.
